title: Introducing Kali Linux
excerpt: In the world of cybersecurity, having the right tools is critical. One of the most widely used operating systems for security professionals and ethical hackers is Kali Linux.
content:
Introduction to Kali Linux: What It Is and Why It Is Used

What Is Kali Linux?

Kali Linux is a Debian-based Linux distribution specifically designed for penetration testing, ethical hacking, and digital forensics.

Why Is Kali Linux Used?

Kali Linux is used to simulate real-world cyber attacks in a controlled and legal environment.

1. Pre-installed Security Tools

Kali includes tools for network scanning, packet analysis, vulnerability assessment, exploitation testing, and web application security testing.

2. Penetration Testing

Security professionals use Kali Linux to legally test systems and networks for vulnerabilities.

3. Digital Forensics

Kali provides tools to analyze compromised systems and recover digital evidence.

4. Wireless Security Testing

It supports wireless network testing and security analysis.

title: Fixing the "Drive Does Not Exist" Error While Installing Antigravity on Windows 11
excerpt: Fixing the "Drive Does Not Exist" Error While Installing Antigravity on Windows 11
content:
Fixing the "Drive Does Not Exist" Error While Installing Antigravity on Windows 11

Recently, I encountered a frustrating issue while reinstalling Antigravity. The installer kept showing:

"The drive or UNC share you selected does not exist or is not accessible."

The problem started after I merged my D: drive into C:. Earlier, Antigravity was installed on D:. Even though the drive was removed, the installer was still referencing the old D:\ path internally. Since that drive no longer existed, the installation failed.

What Was Happening?

When Windows partitions are merged:

  • The old drive letter disappears.
  • Some applications cache the previous install location.
  • The installer may automatically try to reuse that broken path.

Additionally, I downloaded the User Installer version but initially ran it as Administrator, which caused further confusion.

How I Fixed It

  • Opened appwiz.cpl and checked for any existing Antigravity installation.
  • Manually deleted leftover folders from:

C:\Users\Username\AppData\Local
C:\Users\Username\AppData\Roaming
C:\Program Files (if present)

  • Restarted the system.
  • Downloaded a fresh installer.
  • Ran the correct version properly:
    User Installer → normal run
    System Installer → run as Administrator

After completely removing leftover traces and reinstalling correctly, the issue was resolved.

Key Takeaway

If you merge drives or change partitions, always uninstall applications first. Old installation paths can silently break future installs.

Systematic debugging beats random fixes — especially on Windows.
